summary: I was born in Los Angeles in November 1907 and spent my early years there. My family settled in Los Angeles around 1883 during the land boom, which probably offered more opportunities. I lived near Beverly Boulevard and attended local schools like Union Avenue School and Los Angeles High School. Los Angeles has been the backdrop of much of my life and family history.

When I was a child, my family lived just one block north of Beverly Boulevard on Rockwood Street. Beverly Boulevard runs parallel to Glendale Boulevard and stretches from Glendale Boulevard to Union Avenue in Los Angeles. It was part of the neighborhood I grew up in during the 1910s. That area was familiar to me as a kid and part of my daily surroundings.

Glendale Boulevard was one of the streets near where I lived as a child in Los Angeles. Our house on Rockwood Street was just north of Beverly Boulevard, which runs parallel to Glendale Boulevard. This area, including Glendale Boulevard, formed the neighborhood where I spent my early years around 1907 to 1915. It was part of the local geography I knew well growing up.

Union Avenue was another street close to where I lived as a child in Los Angeles. Beverly Boulevard runs from Glendale Boulevard to Union Avenue, so it was part of the same neighborhood. I also attended Union Avenue School, which was located at Union and Beverly, or First Street as it was called then. Union Avenue was a significant part of my childhood environment.

My grandfather came to Austin, Nevada, back in 1869 as a Methodist missionary. That was before my father was born, and it was part of the family's early western journey. After Austin, my grandfather returned to Massachusetts for a while before the family eventually settled in California. Austin, Nevada, marks the beginning of our family's move westward.

Massachusetts was where my grandfather served as a minister for a year or two after his time in Nevada. My father was born in Boston, Massachusetts, though he didn't live there long before the family moved west. Massachusetts represents the family's origins on the East Coast before settling in California. It was an important early chapter in our family's history.

My father was born in Boston, Massachusetts, but he only lived there for a year or two before the family moved west to California. Boston was his birthplace, but he really grew up in California. So while Boston is part of our family story, it wasn't where he spent much of his life.

After leaving Massachusetts, my family settled in Plumas County, California, where my grandfather became a county judge. He also served as a state senator representing Plumas County in Sacramento. This period was an important part of our family's establishment in California before moving down to Los Angeles in the 1880s. Plumas County was where my father grew up and where our family took root.

Sacramento was where my grandfather served as a state senator representing Plumas County. This was before the family moved to Los Angeles in 1883. Sacramento was part of the family's political and professional life in California during the late 19th century. It marked a stage in our family's progression before settling in Southern California.

I attended Union Avenue School for my elementary education, which was located at Union and Beverly, or First Street as it was called then. I went there through the eighth grade, as was common in those days. It was a local school in Los Angeles and part of my early academic experience before moving on to Los Angeles High School.

After finishing at Union Avenue School, I went on to attend Los Angeles High School. It was the one located at Olympic and Rimpau, built around 1919, not the older one on Fort Moore Hill. That school was the only L.A. High at the time I attended, and it was part of my teenage years in Los Angeles. I remember it well, though the building was torn down a year or two ago.

Fort Moore Hill was the location of the older Los Angeles High School building, but I did not attend school there. By the time I went to high school, the new Los Angeles High School had been built at Olympic and Rimpau. So Fort Moore Hill was part of the city's educational history, but not directly part of my schooling experience.
